Q1: I organized my study routine pretty systematically throughout the course. I dedicated about 31 hours each week to studying, spreading it across most days so I wouldn't feel overwhelmed. I'm a visual learner, so I always made sure to have my notes organized with color-coding and diagrams. I'd typically start with watching the lecture videos, then move on to readings, and finally work on assignments. Having a consistent structure really helped me stay on track without feeling stressed.
Q2: I definitely preferred studying at regular times each week. I had specific blocks in my schedule dedicated to this course, usually in the mornings when I felt most focused. Of course, when deadlines approached, I might add an extra session here or there, but I tried not to let things pile up. The regularity helped me maintain my 90% attendance and keep up with all the assignments without last-minute panic.
Q3: I usually logged into the LMS to check for new announcements or to access the week's materials. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to watch lecture videos or to participate in discussion forums. It became almost automatic—like checking email in the morning, I'd check the LMS to see if there was anything new I needed to address. I didn't log in super frequently, maybe a couple times a week, but when I did, I made sure to get everything I needed.
Q4: Yes, using the LMS definitely became part of my normal routine. At the start of each week, I'd log in to download materials and check what was due. It happened naturally because the course was structured in a way that new content appeared weekly, so I knew when to expect things. After a few weeks, it just became habit—Sunday evening or Monday morning, I'd check in and plan my week around what I found there.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I spent a lot of time watching them—probably over two hours worth of content regularly. The discussion forums were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I might have missed. I also appreciated when instructors posted visual summaries or diagrams alongside the readings.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skimmed through the supplementary readings or extra resources that weren't directly tied to assignments. If something felt optional and I was already managing a heavy course load with seven online courses, I had to prioritize. I focused on what would directly help me complete assignments and prepare for exams. It wasn't that those resources weren't valuable, just that time was limited.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to my grades or my understanding of key concepts. If I knew something would appear on an exam or help me complete an assignment better, I engaged with it fully. I also valued activities that were visually engaging or interactive because they suited my learning style. Clear instructions and relevance to course objectives made all the difference.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different from required ones. With required activities, there was a clear sense of purpose and accountability—I knew exactly why I was doing them. Optional ones felt more like extras that I'd get to if I had time, which I often didn't with seven courses. I completed some optional discussion posts because I genuinely found them interesting, but I couldn't prioritize them the same way.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download the lecture slides and PDFs, then review them on my tablet without needing internet. I also created my own visual notes and mind maps based on the materials. Sometimes I'd print things out because highlighting on paper helps me remember better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course content but my LMS activity wouldn't reflect it. For example, I'd watch a video once online, then replay sections from my downloaded copy multiple times. Or I'd spend hours creating summary diagrams from the lecture notes. I also discussed concepts with classmates in person or through messaging apps, which the LMS couldn't track.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying offline for hours. It was more convenient because I could study on my commute or in places without reliable internet. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the three or four times I reviewed the same content offline. My actual engagement was much deeper than the logs would suggest.
Q12: There were a few weeks when balancing seven online courses became challenging, and I felt my attention spreading thin. During those times, it was harder to engage deeply with any single course. I also noticed that when exam periods overlapped, I had to be more strategic about where I put my energy. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me manage these moments and keep my stress low.
Q13: Managing seven courses definitely affected how I used the LMS. I had to be efficient—logging in with purpose rather than browsing aimlessly. When I felt fatigued, I'd prioritize the most urgent tasks and save deeper engagement for when I had more energy. Competing responsibilities meant I couldn't spend unlimited time on any single course, so I developed a system to handle everything systematically.
Q14: Honestly, there were times when I accessed materials but wasn't fully present mentally. Maybe I'd have a video playing while my mind wandered, or I'd scroll through readings without really absorbing them. These moments usually happened when I was tired or had been studying for too long. I learned to recognize when this was happening and would take a break rather than waste time pretending to study.
Q15: I occasionally postponed discussion posts until closer to their deadlines, even though I knew participating earlier would be better. It wasn't avoidance exactly—more like prioritization. With assignments due in multiple courses, I'd handle the graded work first and leave discussions for when I had breathing room. I always completed them eventually, but the timing wasn't always ideal.
Q16: Deadlines were like anchors in my schedule—they helped me structure when to focus on what. I'd work backward from due dates to plan my week, ensuring I had enough time for each assignment. The LMS calendar was useful for this. I rarely missed deadlines because I built buffer time into my routine. Deadlines motivated me to stay consistent rather than causing last-minute stress.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement, though sometimes there was a delay before I received it. When I got feedback on assignments, I'd review it carefully and try to apply those lessons to future work. Positive feedback encouraged me to maintain my approach, while constructive criticism helped me adjust. I appreciated detailed feedback more than just grades because it gave me something actionable.
Q18: The weekly structure of the course really suited my routine-oriented approach. Knowing that new materials would be released at predictable times helped me plan ahead. The clear organization of modules made it easy to track my progress and ensure I wasn't missing anything. I thrived when expectations were laid out clearly from the beginning.
Q19: The visual elements of the course—like video lectures and diagrams—made engagement much easier for me. Having materials organized in a logical sequence also helped. What made things harder was when instructions were unclear or when there was too much text-heavy content without visual breaks. I also found it challenging when discussion prompts were vague and I wasn't sure what kind of response was expected.
Q20: I think students might show high LMS activity without submitting work if they're struggling to understand the material or feeling overwhelmed. They might be accessing resources repeatedly trying to grasp concepts but not feeling confident enough to submit assignments. It could also be anxiety about performance or perfectionism holding them back. Sometimes being active doesn't mean being productive.
Q21: This actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My LMS login frequency was relatively low—just a couple times per week—but I still performed reasonably well because I studied extensively offline. A student might download everything they need in one session and then work independently. Low visible activity doesn't necessarily mean low engagement; it might just mean efficient use of the platform.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like login times, which resources were accessed, and submission timestamps. They give a good overview of behavioral compliance—whether someone is keeping up with deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional investment, and genuine understanding. They can't capture whether someone is truly learning or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actively processing the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm genuinely curious about a topic and want to explore it beyond what's required. It's also about feeling connected to the course—participating in discussions, applying concepts to real life, and feeling motivated to do my best work. Engagement is internal as much as it is behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent creating visual study materials offline or discussing concepts with peers outside the platform. It also wouldn't capture my emotional state—that I felt calm and motivated throughout the course thanks to low stress and the CBT sessions. My genuine interest in certain topics and the satisfaction I felt when understanding difficult concepts are invisible to the data. The numbers tell part of the story, but not the whole picture.
